title: More than Moore - IBM Announces 0.7nm Process Node (Significant logic/SRAM scaling vs 2nm)
gist: IBM has announced a 0.7nm process node featuring a staggered sequential CFET design that achieves 666 million transistors per square millimeter, with 40% SRAM scaling and 50% logic scaling compared to IBM's 2nm node. IBM is also acquiring a High-NA EUV machine, signaling continued commitment to Moore's Law-style scaling. The announcement contrasts with major commercial foundries' approaches to next-generation transistor architectures.

insights.claim: IBM has announced a 0.7nm process node achieving 666 million transistors per square millimeter, representing a significant density leap.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: IBM's density milestone at 0.7nm signals continued viability of scaling, relevant to investors tracking semiconductor R&D trajectories and potential licensing or foundry partnerships.

insights.claim: IBM's 0.7nm node delivers 40% SRAM scaling and 50% logic scaling compared to IBM's 2nm process node.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Substantial logic and SRAM area reduction at 0.7nm would improve chip economics and performance-per-watt, key metrics for AI accelerator and compute-dense workloads.

insights.claim: IBM has built a staggered sequential CFET (Complementary FET) design, one of multiple CFET variants described in the academic literature.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: CFET architecture choices will determine manufacturability and yield at sub-1nm nodes; IBM's specific variant selection differentiates its roadmap from Intel, TSMC, and Samsung.
insights.quote: IBM has built a staggered sequential CFET design

insights.claim: IBM is acquiring a High-NA EUV lithography machine, aligning it with the most advanced patterning tool available for sub-2nm manufacturing.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: High-NA EUV procurement is a major capital commitment; IBM joining Intel and TSMC/Samsung in High-NA adoption strengthens the case that sub-1nm nodes will reach production-readiness this decade.
insights.quote: IBM is getting a High-NA machine

insights.claim: IBM's roadmap reflects a belief that Moore's Law remains alive, in contrast to foundries pursuing alternative scaling strategies.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: If IBM's scaling thesis is validated, it could pressure commercial foundries (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) to accelerate CFET timelines and challenge the 'More than Moore' diversification narrative.
insights.quote: When it comes to IBM, I firmly believe they're in the camp of saying Moore's Law is still alive.

insights.claim: A detailed analysis by Ian Cuttress contrasts major chip foundry approaches to future transistor types with IBM's 0.7nm NanoStack architecture, supported by charts and diagrams.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Comparative foundry analysis helps investors assess which players have credible sub-1nm roadmaps and are likely to capture advanced-node market share through the late 2020s.
